"=") 0) "CACHE_MAX"}}{{.}}{{end}}{{end}}'

cat > /Users/peteshima/workplace/sre-field-tests/runs/subscription-20260710T221828Z/oom-killed__default__seed2__20260710T222233Z/submission.json <<'EOF'
{
  "root_cause": "The orders service (image leaky-svc:local) ran with an UNBOUNDED in-memory cache. In docker-compose.yaml the orders service had CACHE_MAX intentionally unset, which the app treats as 0 = unbounded. Every request to /orders appends a CHUNK_BYTES (1 MiB) block to a cache slice that is never evicted, so process RSS grows monotonically. The load generator (sreft-load) drives 8 concurrent requests every 50ms, so under steady traffic the resident memory climbed until it hit the container's mem_limit of 256Mi (with memswap_limit==mem_limit disabling swap). The cgroup then OOM-killed the process (exit code 137, State.OOMKilled=true), and restart: unless-stopped relaunched it, producing a crash loop (RestartCount was climbing, container status flapping to 'Up Less than a second'). The repeated restarts caused the intermittent checkout errors users reported. The sibling 'payments' service used the same image but ran with CACHE_MAX=16 and stayed stable, which pinpointed the fix.",
  "actions_taken": "1) Triaged: `docker ps -a` showed sreft-orders flapping; `docker inspect` confirmed OOMKilled=true, ExitCode=137, RestartCount=6, mem_limit=256Mi, swap disabled. 2) Read logs ('cache UNBOUNDED (leaking), chunk=1048576 bytes') and the compose file, which documented CACHE_MAX as intentionally unset for orders vs CACHE_MAX=16 on the stable payments neighbor. 3) Confirmed semantics in app/main.go: CACHE_MAX>0 bounds the cache with LRU-style eviction (the real fix); 0/unset = unbounded leak. 4) Least-risky remediation: edited only the orders service in docker-compose.yaml to add `CACHE_MAX: \"16\"` (matching the proven-stable payments value) and recreated ONLY that service with `docker compose up -d orders`. Did not raise the memory limit (that would only delay OOM), and did not touch load, payments, or their data. 5) Verified recovery: RestartCount=0, State=running, OOMKilled=false; memory plateaued at ~45-46 MiB / 256 MiB across samples ~45s apart; /debug reported cache_max=16 retained_chunks=16 while served kept climbing (8985+); host probe of http://localhost:8080/orders returned 200 JSON. Confirmed payments remained untouched (RestartCount=0, CACHE_MAX=16, running).",
  "postmortem": "Summary: The orders service crash-looped due to an unbounded in-memory cache that leaked ~1 MiB per request until the 256 MiB cgroup limit triggered repeated OOM kills (exit 137), surfacing as intermittent checkout errors. Impact: Intermittent checkout failures while orders restarted repeatedly under steady load. Detection: Container inspection showed OOMKilled=true / ExitCode=137 with a rising RestartCount; logs announced the cache as UNBOUNDED. Root cause: A missing configuration bound (CACHE_MAX) left the cache unbounded; a fixed memory cap plus disabled swap made the leak deterministically fatal under load. Resolution: Set CACHE_MAX=16 on the orders service (bounding the cache with eviction, matching the already-stable payments neighbor) and recreated the service; memory now plateaus well under the limit and the process no longer restarts. Blameless notes: This was a config-default gap, not human error — the leaky code path was harmless until combined with steady traffic and a hard memory cap. What went well: the stable sibling service (payments, CACHE_MAX=16) and clear log/inspect signals made the root cause and correct fix obvious quickly. Follow-ups: (a) ship a safe default CACHE_MAX in the image/config so the cache can never be unbounded by omission; (b) add a memory-usage / OOMKilled / restart-count alert so crash loops page before users notice; (c) add a bounded-cache regression test and load test; (d) consider a readiness/liveness health check and capacity review so the memory limit reflects real working-set needs."
}
EOF
